text: The bat mesh. A laparoscopic bilateral transabdominal pre-parentonial inguinal hernia repair with a single mesh. We present in this video the case of a fifty six year old male patient who presented with bilateral painful inguinal hernias. The patient past medical history showed no previous intervention. We decided to perform a laparoscopic bilateral transabdominal preperitoneal repair with a single mesh, the so-called bat mesh.

The surgeon displays a bat-shaped polypropylene mesh measuring 29 by 12 centimeters, cut from a rectangular monofilament standard weight polypropylene prosthesis. The mesh is folded in half before being shaped.

The surgeon uses a 12mm peri-umbilical optical trocar and two 5mm trocars to explore the abdomen and inguinal fossae. On the right side, a direct and an indirect hernia are identified, with the indirect hernia already reduced. The peritoneum is opened, and the right direct hernia defect is exposed. On the left side, adhesions between the sigmoid colon and abdominal wall are sharply dissected, the peritoneum is opened, and the left direct hernia defect is revealed.

The surgeon performs medial dissection in the retropubic space, creating a retropubic tunnel. A rolled-up polypropylene mesh is introduced through the 12mm optical trocar and grasped with laparoscopic forceps. The mesh is passed from the left side through the retropubic tunnel to the right side using a reabsorbable stay suture. The mesh is then deployed and oriented with reabsorbable stitches of varying lengths used as landmarks.

The surgeon secures the bat-shaped mesh in place by placing tacks on both sides and at the center of the prosthesis, ensuring it covers all hernia defects. The retropubic portion of the mesh aids in preventing dislocation. The peritoneum is then closed with a running barbed reabsorbable suture.

The operative time was one hundred twenty minutes. The postoperative hospital stay was regular and the patient was dismissed on postoperative day one. Four months after the operation the patient was completely asymptomatic.
